name: Beaker Set
description: Graduated beakers let scientists measure and mix liquids precisely. Every real lab starts here.

name: Magnifying Glass
description: A convex lens that bends light to enlarge small details, used by naturalists for centuries.

name: Digital Thermometer
description: Measures temperature by sensing how materials change with heat. Essential for chemistry and biology.

name: Precision Scale
description: Accurate to a tenth of a gram. Careful measurement is what separates an experiment from a guess.

name: Bunsen Burner
description: The classic lab heat source, invented in 1855. Controlled flames enable reactions and sterilization.

name: Plant Growth Station
description: Grow lights and trays for botany experiments. Track how light, water, and soil change plant growth.

name: Electromagnet Kit
description: Coils, cores, and compasses for exploring the invisible force fields that power motors and generators.

name: Compound Microscope
description: Stacks two lenses to magnify up to 1000x, revealing cells, microbes, and crystal structures.

name: Circuit Workbench
description: Breadboards, LEDs, and resistors for building real electronics without soldering.

name: Chemistry Fume Hood
description: Ventilated workspace that safely carries fumes away. Safety equipment makes bolder experiments possible.

name: Refractor Telescope
description: Gathers far more light than your eye, bringing the Moon's craters and Jupiter's moons into view.

name: Ecosystem Aquarium
description: A self-contained water ecosystem for studying food webs, nitrogen cycles, and animal behavior.

name: Weather Station
description: Barometer, anemometer, and rain gauge. Meteorologists use these instruments to forecast storms.

name: Robotic Arm
description: Programmable servos and joints, like the robots that assemble cars and explore Mars.

name: 3D Printer
description: Builds objects layer by layer from melted plastic. Engineers prototype ideas overnight with these.

name: Laser Optics Bench
description: Mirrors, prisms, and beam splitters for exploring how light reflects, refracts, and interferes.

name: DNA Sequencer
description: Reads the genetic code letter by letter. Sequencing the first human genome took 13 years; now it takes hours.

name: Wind Tunnel
description: Blows controlled air over models to test aerodynamics, just like NASA and race car designers.

name: Spectrometer
description: Splits light into its spectrum to identify what things are made of, even distant stars.

name: Supercomputer Cluster
description: Thousands of processors working together to simulate weather, molecules, and galaxies.

name: Electron Microscope
description: Uses electron beams instead of light to see objects a million times smaller than a millimeter.

name: Tesla Coil
description: A resonant transformer that hurls artificial lightning. Nikola Tesla built the first one in 1891.

name: Clean Room Module
description: An ultra-filtered room with almost zero dust, where microchips and spacecraft parts are assembled.

name: Planetarium Dome
description: A projection dome that recreates the night sky from anywhere on Earth, at any point in history.
